title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Type 1 Credit Prices Dip Marginally; Trading Volumes Soar Over 2.74x WoW
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(14th â€“ 20th July 2025)
  • A total of 567,033 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 273.76% rise week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a 39.33% fall WoW as 1,423,669 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $44.45/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 0.96% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 16.43% in price, reaching $54.62/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 6.96% WoW; on 22nd July, the spot price stood at $53.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $56.25/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices remained unchanged last week. D4, D5 prices rose by 5.50%, while D6 RIN prices increased by 7.41% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

In the past week, prices for Type 1 trades fell marginally, while spot prices rose after recording a decline last week. A new incentive program has been introduced in Washington state to accelerate the adoption of zero-emission trucks and charging infrastructure for drayage operations in the Puget Sound region. The initiative is spearheaded by the Northwest Seaport Alliance (NWSA) and is supported by a $6.2 million grant from the Washington State Department of Transportation (WSDOT). Â The initiative is part of Washingtonâ€™s Climate Commitment Act, aiming to decarbonize port operations, cut air pollution, and support environmental justice in communities near ports The Washington Department of Ecology held online public hearings on 22nd July on the Â on the Clean Fuel Standard rulemaking, which included presentations, Q&A sessions, and opportunities for public comment. Written comments shall also accepted online or by mail, and are to be submitted by 1st August 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.96% WoW fall, decreasing from $44.88 to $44.45 this week, and all non-zero credit prices decreased by 16.43% WoW, from $65.36 to $54.62. Type 1 trades saw 567,033 credits exchanged, reflecting a 2.74x times rise WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw a 39.33% fall WoW, reaching 1,423,669 credits. [caption id="attachment_600988" align="alignnone" width="802"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 6.96% WoW, reaching $47.33/MT. On 22nd July, the spot price was $53.00/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $56.25/MT as of 22nd July. [caption id="attachment_600987" align="alignnone" width="802"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

As per the data reported by CARB last week, 102 trades were reported in the market, with 10 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $100.00 for two Type 2 transactions at a volume of 14,245 credits and 15,140 credits, respectively. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $40.00 per credit of one Type 1 transaction reported for 50,000 credits. The smallest volume transfers of 39 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $46.00, while the largest volume transfers of 200,000 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken at $44.00. [caption id="attachment_600985" align="alignnone" width="800"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

The average spot Type 1 price stood at $44.90 in July so far, up from $44.06 in June. Trade volumes for the month of June stood at 4.89 M, while those for July stood at 4.31 M as of 20th July, 2025. [caption id="attachment_600984" align="alignnone" width="800"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 14th Â July â€“ 20th July, total VPT recorded a fall of 24.71% WoW, with a fall in all non-zero credit prices for the last week. [caption id="attachment_600983" align="alignnone" width="801"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] Â Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices remained unchanged last week. D4, D5 prices rose by 5.50%, while D6 RIN prices increased by 7.41% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming Dates

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this Week

  • USTR Announces Initiation of Section 301 Investigation of Brazilâ€™s Unfair Trading Practices: The Office of the United States Trade Representative initiated an investigation of Brazil under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974. The investigation will seek to determine whether acts, policies, and practices of the Government of Brazil related to digital trade and electronic payment services; unfair, preferential tariffs; anti-corruption interference; intellectual property protection; ethanol market access; and illegal deforestation are unreasonable or discriminatory and burden or restrict U.S. commerce.
  • WattEV Accelerates Freight Electrification with Three New Megawatt Charging Depots in Southern California: WattEV will install 29 megawatt chargers across Otay Mesa (7), Baker (10), and the Port of Long Beach (12), supporting up to 100,000 annual charging sessions and enabling <30-minute electric truck refueling. Backed by $24M from the California Transportation Commission, the sites use proprietary SST technology and support WattEVâ€™s TaaS model, which delivers 200,000+ zero-emission freight miles monthly.
  • Hallcon Breaks Ground on Washingtonâ€™s Largest EV Fleet Charging Facility: Hallcon Corporation, a leading provider of transportation and infrastructure services in North America, today announced the groundbreaking of its newest Electric Vehicle Operations Center (EVOC) in Redmond, WA. As the largest 24/7 EV fleet charging facility in Washington, this state-of-the-art facility marks a significant step forward in sustainable transportation infrastructure, meeting the growing demand for clean mobility solutions in the Puget Sound region.
